rationale: This is a classic textbook example of the Houben-Hoesch reaction, demonstrating the acylation of a highly activated, electron-rich aromatic ring (1,3,5-trimethoxybenzene) with a simple nitrile (acetonitrile). The reaction is catalyzed by a Lewis acid (ZnCl2) and a protic acid (HCl). The product is an aryl ketone (2,4,6-trimethoxyacetophenone). This example clearly illustrates the key transformation: the formation of a C(aryl)-C(acyl) bond from an arene and a nitrile.
collision_avoidance_note: This example uses a simple, highly activated arene (1,3,5-trimethoxybenzene) and a simple nitrile (acetonitrile), which are characteristic of the Houben-Hoesch reaction. It avoids using phenol itself, which could be confused with the Gattermann reaction (which uses HCN or its equivalents). It also avoids complex structures or other functional groups that might trigger unrelated named reaction queries like Friedel-Crafts acylation (which typically uses acyl halides or anhydrides) or Fries rearrangement.
